title: Filing Domestic Violence Complaint
description: Step-by-step guide to file a domestic violence complaint under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005.

steps.details: If you are in immediate danger, call 100 (Police) or 181 (Women Helpline). Move to a safe location — a relative's house, friend's place, or contact a shelter home through the Women Helpline.

Collect any evidence: photos of injuries, threatening messages/recordings, medical reports from hospital visits, witness statements from neighbours or family. Even without evidence, you can file a complaint.

Every district has a designated Protection Officer under the DV Act. Contact them through the District Women & Child Development office, or through Women Helpline 181. The Protection Officer will help you file the complaint.

The Protection Officer will help you fill out the Domestic Incident Report form. This is a detailed form documenting the violence — physical, emotional, economic, or sexual. Be as specific as possible about incidents.

With the Protection Officer's help, file an application before the Magistrate Court under Section 12 of the DV Act. You can seek: Protection Order, Residence Order, Monetary Relief, Custody Order, and Compensation.

The Magistrate must issue notice to the respondent and schedule a hearing. You can request an ex-parte order (without the abuser being present) in urgent cases. Free legal aid is available through DLSA.
steps.timeline: First hearing within 3 days of application (for urgent matters)

steps.details: If the court grants a Protection Order, the respondent is legally prohibited from committing any act of violence, entering your workplace, or contacting you. Violation of this order is punishable with jail up to 1 year.

relevant_authorities.name: Protection Officer
relevant_authorities.contact: Available at District Women & Child Development Office

estimated_timeline: Protection Order: 3-60 days. Full case resolution: 2-6 months.
tips: You do NOT need to file a police FIR first — you can directly approach the Protection Officer or Magistrate
Domestic violence includes emotional abuse, economic deprivation, and threats — not just physical violence
You have the right to continue living in the shared household even if the property is not in your name
Free legal aid is available through DLSA — you don't need to hire a private lawyer
Keep a diary of incidents with dates and details — this strengthens your case
